name: Time and Work

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - 18-question real past-paper set on Time and Work

researchNotes: This is the 10th and FINAL named Aptitude skill, completing all of Part B Unit I. 18 real past-paper questions found on one source, covering many distinct shapes — picked ~9 representative ones spanning men-days inverse proportion, multi-worker combinations, pipes (fill+drain), wages-by-work-share, and two classic 'trap' questions (the spider-web problem and the persons-projects-days problem, both designed to trick you into multiplying instead of recognizing the ratio stays constant). Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only — also used Day 10, already completed): added the efficiency-ratio method (converting 'twice as efficient' into a rate ratio), the LCM-as-total-work-units shortcut (avoids fraction arithmetic entirely), efficiency-based wage-splitting (distinct from the existing work-fraction-based split), and one more classic ratio trap. Every question, new and pre-existing, now carries the mandatory Part-B `trick`.

reading.intro: Time and Work is the last of the 10 named Aptitude skills — after this, Part B moves to its Reasoning unit. Real exam evidence shows heavy use of 'trap' questions designed to look like they need complex math but actually resolve via a simple ratio.

qa.question: Method — inverse proportion (more workers, less time): 12 men complete a work in 36 days. In how many days will 18 men finish the same work?
qa.answer: Men × Days = constant (total work). 12×36 = 18×x → x = 432/18 = 24 days. More workers means proportionally fewer days.

qa.question: Method — sequential combined work: A, B, C can complete a job alone in 8, 12, 16 days respectively. A and C work together for 2 days, then C leaves and B joins A. In how many more days do A and B finish?
qa.answer: Work rates: A=1/8, B=1/12, C=1/16 per day. In 2 days, A+C complete 2×(1/8+1/16)=2×(3/16)=3/8 of the work. Remaining = 5/8. A+B's combined rate = 1/8+1/12 = 5/24 per day. Days needed = (5/8)÷(5/24) = (5/8)×(24/5) = 3 days.

qa.question: Method — wages split by share of work done: A can do a job in 10 days, B in 15 days. They work together for 5 days, then C finishes the remaining work in 2 days. Total payment for the whole job is Rs.5,000 — how should it be split?
qa.answer: Pay is split in proportion to how much of the TOTAL work each person actually did, not how many days they worked. In 5 days, A completes 5/10=1/2 of the job, B completes 5/15=1/3; together that's 5/6, leaving 1/6 for C to finish. Split of Rs.5,000 by work share: A gets 1/2×5000=Rs.2,500; B gets 1/3×5000≈Rs.1,667; C gets 1/6×5000≈Rs.833. The key insight is the split follows WORK DONE, not days worked — C worked fewer days than B but did a comparable or different share depending on the rates, and pay tracks output, not time.

qa.question: Method — pairwise combined rates → solve for all three together: A+B finish a job in 12 days, B+C in 15 days, C+A in 20 days. In how many days can A, B, C finish it together?
qa.answer: Add all three pairwise rates: (A+B)+(B+C)+(C+A) = 1/12+1/15+1/20 = 2(A+B+C)'s rate. LCM=60: 5/60+4/60+3/60=12/60=1/5. So 2×(A+B+C rate)=1/5 → (A+B+C) rate=1/10 → together they finish in 10 days.

qa.question: Trap question: If 9 spiders make 9 webs in 9 days, how many days does 1 spider take to make 1 web?
qa.answer: 9 days — NOT 1 day. Each spider makes 1 web in 9 days regardless of how many spiders work in parallel (they're not collaborating on the SAME web). This tests whether you recognize the rate-per-individual stays fixed, the classic trap being to assume the answer scales down to 1.

qa.question: Method — pipes filling and draining together: Two pipes fill a tank in 30 and 40 minutes separately; a third pipe drains it in 24 minutes. With all three open, how long to fill the tank?
qa.answer: Combined rate = 1/30+1/40−1/24 (subtract the draining pipe's rate). LCM=120: 4/120+3/120−5/120 = 2/120 = 1/60. So the tank fills in 60 minutes.

qa.question: Method — provisions lasting fewer days with more people (inverse proportion again): 1,000 soldiers have 70 days of provisions. If 400 more soldiers join, how long will provisions last?
qa.answer: People × Days = constant (total provisions). 1000×70 = 1400×x → x = 70000/1400 = 50 days.

qa.question: Method — find the second worker's solo time from a combined time and the first worker's solo time: A and B together finish a job in 16 days; A alone takes 48 days. How long does B alone take?
qa.answer: B's rate = (A+B)'s rate − A's rate = 1/16 − 1/48 = 3/48−1/48 = 2/48 = 1/24. So B alone takes 24 days.

qa.question: Trap question: If 5 persons complete 5 projects in 5 days, how long do 50 persons take to complete 50 projects?
qa.answer: Still 5 days — NOT 50 days. The ratio of people to projects stays the same (1:1), so the per-project time doesn't change; 50 people working on 50 projects is just 10 independent copies of the original 5-person/5-project scenario happening in parallel.

qa.question: Method — efficiency ratio shortcut: If A is twice as efficient as B, and together they complete a job in 12 days, how long would A alone take?
qa.answer: Efficiency ratio A:B = 2:1, so combined rate = 3 'units' per day over 12 days = 36 total units of work. A's rate = 2 units/day, so A alone takes 36/2 = 18 days.
qa.tip: Convert an efficiency ratio directly into a rate ratio (e.g. 2:1), find total work from the combined rate × combined time, then divide by each person's individual rate share — avoids ever writing a fraction.

qa.question: Method — using LCM as 'total work units' to avoid fractions: A can finish a job in 20 days, B in 30 days. Using total work = LCM(20,30) = 60 units, find how long they take together.
qa.answer: Total work = 60 units (LCM of 20 and 30). A's rate = 60/20 = 3 units/day. B's rate = 60/30 = 2 units/day. Combined rate = 5 units/day. Together: 60/5 = 12 days.
qa.tip: Set total work = LCM of all the given solo-completion times — this converts every rate into a whole number of 'units per day', eliminating fraction arithmetic throughout the whole problem.

qa.question: Method — wage-splitting by EFFICIENCY ratio (when both work the same number of days, unlike the work-fraction-split method above): A is thrice as efficient as B. If they earn Rs.800 together for a job finished by working the SAME number of days, how is the pay split?
qa.answer: Since both work the same number of days, pay splits directly by efficiency ratio 3:1. A gets (3/4)×800 = Rs.600; B gets (1/4)×800 = Rs.200.
qa.tip: Only split pay by WORK-FRACTION-ACTUALLY-DONE when people work different numbers of days (like r03's example); when everyone works the SAME number of days, pay splits directly by efficiency/rate ratio instead — don't confuse the two methods.

questions.prompt: 15 men complete a work in 24 days. In how many days will 20 men finish the same work?
questions.trick: Men × Days = constant total work — a direct inverse-proportion setup.

questions.prompt: A and B together can finish a job in 18 days; A alone takes 45 days. How long does B alone take?
questions.trick: B's rate = (combined rate) − (A's rate) — always subtract rates, never subtract the raw day-counts directly.

questions.prompt: If 7 workers build 7 walls in 7 days, how many days does 1 worker take to build 1 wall?
questions.explanation: 7 days — each worker's individual rate is unaffected by how many work in parallel on separate walls.
questions.trick: Whenever people-count = task-count in these 'N people, N tasks, N days' questions, the answer is always just N days (or whatever the given day-count is) — the scaling is an illusion.

questions.prompt: Two pipes fill a tank in 12 and 15 minutes separately; a third pipe drains it in 20 minutes. With all three open, how long to fill the tank?
questions.trick: Filling pipes ADD their rates, draining pipes SUBTRACT — combine all rates with correct signs before inverting for the final time.

questions.prompt: 800 soldiers have provisions for 60 days. If 200 more soldiers join, how long will the provisions last?
questions.trick: People × Days = constant total provisions — same inverse-proportion pattern as men-and-work problems.

questions.prompt: A, B, and C can do a job alone in 24, 30, and 40 days respectively. Working together, how long will they take?
questions.trick: Add individual rates (1/days for each), then invert the sum for the combined time.

questions.prompt: If 8 people complete 8 tasks in 8 days, how long do 40 people take to complete 40 tasks?
questions.explanation: Still 8 days — the people-to-task ratio (1:1) is unchanged, so per-task time is unchanged.
questions.trick: Same people:task ratio → same per-task time, regardless of how much everything is scaled up together.

questions.prompt: A is twice as efficient as B. Together they finish a job in 12 days. How long would A alone take?
questions.explanation: Ratio 2:1, combined rate 3 units/day ×12 days = 36 total units. A's rate=2/day → 36/2=18 days.
questions.trick: Convert 'twice as efficient' straight into a rate ratio (2:1), then find total work from combined rate × combined time.

questions.prompt: Using total-work-as-LCM: A can finish a job in 20 days, B in 30 days. How long will they take together?
questions.explanation: Total work=LCM(20,30)=60. A=3/day, B=2/day, combined=5/day. 60/5=12 days.
questions.trick: Set total work = LCM of the given day-counts to work entirely in whole numbers, no fractions.

questions.prompt: A is thrice as efficient as B. Working the same number of days together, they earn Rs.800. How much does A get?
questions.trick: Equal days worked → split pay by efficiency ratio directly, not by a work-fraction calculation.

questions.prompt: A can do a job in 15 days, B in 20 days. They work together for 5 days. What fraction of the job remains?
questions.explanation: Combined rate=1/15+1/20=7/60. In 5 days: 35/60=7/12 done. Remaining=5/12.
questions.trick: Find combined rate first, multiply by days worked to get fraction DONE, then subtract from 1 for the fraction remaining.

questions.prompt: A does a job in 6 days that B can do in 8 days. Working together, how much of the job is completed in 2 days?
questions.trick: Combined rate × number of days = fraction of work done — the direct formula, no need to find the remaining fraction here.

questions.prompt: If 6 cats catch 6 mice in 6 minutes, how many minutes will 100 cats take to catch 100 mice?
questions.explanation: Still 6 minutes — the cat-to-mice ratio (1:1) is unchanged.
questions.trick: Classic ratio trap: when the ratio of workers to tasks doesn't change, neither does the per-task time — ignore the scaled-up numbers entirely.

questions.prompt: A can complete a work in 18 days. B is 50% more efficient than A. How many days will B alone take?
questions.explanation: B's rate=1.5×A's rate=1.5/18=1/12. B takes 12 days.
questions.trick: '50% more efficient' means rate ×1.5 — apply the multiplier to the RATE, then invert for B's own days.

questions.prompt: A and B together complete a job in 15 days. If A alone takes 25 days, how long does B alone take?
questions.trick: B's rate = combined rate − A's rate; use a common denominator (here 75) to subtract cleanly.
